title: Three weeks of greasy, foul diarrhea and weight loss in a man with diabetes and hypothyroidism

chart.cc: Prolonged diarrhea, bloating, and weight loss
chart.oneLiner: 44M with type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, and reflux (on metformin, levothyroxine, and a long-term PPI) presents with about three weeks of greasy, foul-smelling, floating diarrhea, marked bloating, sulfur burps, cramps, and roughly 10 lb of weight loss after a backcountry camping trip; he has felt weak and his recent labs show a rising TSH.

opening: I've had this awful greasy diarrhea for about three weeks now — it smells terrible, I'm bloated and gassy all the time, and I've lost almost ten pounds. Lately I just feel weak and washed out.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 44-year-old man, fatigued and a bit thinner than usual, mildly embarrassed about the gas and smell. Reasonable historian but worried because he keeps losing weight despite the doctor recently adjusting his thyroid pill.

HPI: About 3 weeks of loose, greasy, pale, foul-smelling, frequently floating stools (4–7x/day), with marked bloating, belching, sulfur-tasting ('rotten egg') burps, crampy abdominal pain, and a lot of flatulence. Reports roughly 10 lb of weight loss and poor appetite. Symptoms are subacute and persistent (NOT acute and self-limited). Feels generally weak, with mild muscle cramps and palpitations over the last week (only mention the weakness/cramps/palpitations if asked about other symptoms, energy, or the heart). Went on a week-long backcountry fishing/camping trip ~4 weeks ago and drank water from a mountain stream filtered only with a basic pump filter, not boiled or chemically treated (only if asked about travel/outdoor exposure/water source). No daycare contact but helps care for a toddler granddaughter on weekends and changes her diapers (only if asked about sick contacts/childcare). NO blood in stool, NO mucus, NO high fever, NO nocturnal diarrhea waking him, NO recent antibiotics, NO international travel, NO known immunosuppression/HIV risk factors. Dairy now makes the bloating and gas much worse — milk and ice cream really set him off, more than before (only if asked about diet/triggers).

PMH: Type 2 diabetes (~6 years, last A1c ~7.2%); hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's, on replacement for years); GERD with long-standing reflux. A primary-care visit two weeks ago checked labs and found his TSH had crept up, so his levothyroxine dose was increased, but he does not feel better. MEDS: metformin 1000 mg twice daily; levothyroxine 100 mcg daily (recently increased from 88 mcg); omeprazole 40 mg daily (on it for years for reflux); occasional ibuprofen for back pain. He often takes the levothyroxine WITH breakfast and his coffee (only mention timing if asked how he takes his thyroid pill). NO recent antibiotics. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: drinks 3–4 beers on weekends; non-smoker; works as an electrician; monogamous, no high-risk sexual exposures. FAMILY: a sister has celiac disease (only if asked about family history of bowel disease/celiac). No known IBD.

ROS: as above — fatigue, weakness, mild muscle cramps, occasional palpitations; no chest pain, no syncope, no fevers/chills, no rashes, no joint pains, no melena/hematochezia.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: fatigued, slightly thin, mildly pale; afebrile, no acute distress.
- Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 88, RR 16, 37.1°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Mucous membranes / skin: mucous membranes slightly dry; skin turgor mildly reduced; no severe dehydration.
- Conjunctiva/palms: mild conjunctival pallor.
- Abdomen: soft, mildly distended/bloated with hyperactive bowel sounds and mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.
- Neuro: mildly diminished vibration sense at the great toes; no focal deficit (reveal only if a neuro/sensory exam is done).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the camping trip or untreated stream water unless asked about travel, the outdoors, or water source/exposures.
- Do NOT volunteer the toddler granddaughter unless asked about sick contacts or childcare exposure.
- Do NOT volunteer the worsened dairy intolerance unless asked about diet or what makes symptoms worse.
- Do NOT volunteer the family history of celiac unless asked about family bowel/celiac disease.
- Do NOT volunteer the weakness, muscle cramps, or palpitations unless asked about energy/other symptoms or the heart.
- Do NOT volunteer how/when you take the levothyroxine unless asked specifically about the thyroid medication or its timing.
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer. Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.StoolOvaParasites: Stool ova & parasites: Giardia lamblia trophozoites and cysts seen on microscopy (sensitivity improves with repeat sampling; intermittent cyst shedding).
labs.StoolGiardiaAntigen: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA/immunoassay) POSITIVE — confirms giardiasis (more sensitive than a single O&P).
labs.StoolCultureAndPanel: Routine bacterial stool culture NEGATIVE; multiplex GI pathogen panel POSITIVE for Giardia, NEGATIVE for Salmonella/Shigella/Campylobacter/STEC/Cryptosporidium; fecal elastase normal (exocrine pancreatic function intact).
labs.FecalLeukocytesAndCdiff: Fecal leukocytes ABSENT and fecal occult blood NEGATIVE (non-inflammatory diarrhea); C. difficile toxin NEGATIVE; stool fat qualitatively increased (steatorrhea consistent with small-bowel malabsorption).
labs.CBC: WBC 6.8 (no eosinophilia), Hgb 11.4 with MCV 104 (macrocytic anemia), Plt 240 — no leukocytosis.
labs.BMP: Na 137, K 2.9 (low), Cl 110 (high), HCO3 17 (low), BUN 22, Cr 1.0, glucose 148. Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 137 − (110 + 17) = 10 → a NORMAL anion gap; this is a hypokalemic, hyperchloremic (NON-anion-gap) metabolic acidosis from GI bicarbonate and potassium losses.
labs.MicronutrientPanel: Vitamin B12 LOW at 165 pg/mL; 25-OH vitamin D LOW at 14 ng/mL; magnesium 1.5 (low-normal); albumin 3.4 — pattern of fat-soluble-vitamin and B12 malabsorption (long-term PPI and diarrhea both contribute to low B12).
labs.ThyroidAndCeliac: TSH 9.8 (HIGH) with low-normal free T4 despite a recent levothyroxine dose increase — under-replacement from impaired absorption. tTG-IgA mildly elevated at 24 U/mL (weak positive; total IgA normal) — a non-specific finding that can occur with giardiasis-related mucosal injury and must be re-checked after treatment.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm ~88 bpm with flattened T waves, prominent U waves (best seen in V2–V3), mild ST depression, and a long apparent QU interval — the classic EKG signature of hypokalemia; no acute ischemia.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus rhythm with flat T waves, prominent U waves, ST depression and long QU interval consistent with hypokalemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing flattened T waves with prominent U waves and ST depression consistent with hypokalemia
searchTerms: U waves flat T waves hypokalemia 12 lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 44-year-old man with three weeks of greasy malabsorptive diarrhea, weakness, and palpitations has an EKG obtained because of his electrolyte abnormalities.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no free air under the diaphragm, no consolidation or effusion.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: normal PA chest radiograph with no acute process
searchTerms: normal chest radiograph no acute process
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 44-year-old man with chronic loose stools and weight loss has a screening film performed during his malabsorption workup.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this prolonged greasy diarrhea with weight loss (note your leading diagnosis). Also name three mechanisms by which a small-bowel process like this produces malabsorptive/steatorrheic diarrhea.
steps.rubric: Giardiasis / protozoal parasitic diarrhea after untreated water — leading (leading,6). Celiac disease / sprue — genuine competitor given malabsorption, macrocytosis, low B12/vitamin D, weakly positive tTG, and a sister with celiac (CANT-MISS,5). Inflammatory bowel disease — Crohn's (CANT-MISS,3). Other parasites (Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora) or small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth, and chronic pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (core,3). Three mechanisms of malabsorptive/steatorrheic diarrhea — e.g. mucosal villous injury/reduced absorptive surface, osmotic load from unabsorbed solute (incl. secondary lactase deficiency), and fat malabsorption from impaired micelle handling/luminal disruption (core,5). Frames a non-inflammatory malabsorptive small-bowel diarrhea and the need to exclude celiac and IBD (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on giardiasis and ignoring celiac/malabsorption mimics (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Giardiasis (subacute non-inflammatory malabsorptive diarrhea after untreated stream water, with sulfur burps and floating stools). Genuine competing must-not-miss: Celiac disease — it produces the same malabsorptive picture (steatorrhea, weight loss, macrocytosis, low B12/vitamin D), he has a weakly positive tTG-IgA and a sister with celiac. Also must-not-miss: inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's). Also reasonable: other parasites (Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora), small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth, chronic pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, and secondary lactose intolerance. Three mechanisms of malabsorptive/steatorrheic diarrhea: (1) mucosal villous injury reducing absorptive surface area; (2) an osmotic load from unabsorbed solute (including secondary lactase deficiency) drawing water into the lumen; (3) fat malabsorption from disrupted micelle formation/luminal digestion producing steatorrhea.

criticalActions.item: Celiac disease / sprue as a genuine competing malabsorptive diagnosis

coreActions.item: Other parasites / SIBO / pancreatic exocrine insufficiency
Names three mechanisms of malabsorptive/steatorrheic diarrhea (villous injury, osmotic load, fat malabsorption)
Frames a non-inflammatory malabsorptive small-bowel diarrhea needing celiac and IBD exclusion

penalties.item: Anchors on giardiasis and ignores celiac/malabsorption mimics

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites (CANT-MISS,4). BMP — he has weakness/palpitations and likely electrolyte losses; check K/HCO3/anion gap (CANT-MISS,3). Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel +/- fecal elastase to exclude bacterial causes and pancreatic insufficiency (core,2). Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin to characterize as non-inflammatory (core,2). Celiac serology (tTG-IgA with total IgA) given the malabsorptive/macrocytic picture and family history (core,2). CBC and micronutrients — B12, vitamin D, magnesium — and TSH to explain macrocytosis, malabsorption, and the rising thyroid level (core,3). EKG given hypokalemia symptoms (core,2). CXR (core,1). PENALTY: empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing (-2); CT/colonoscopy first-line before basic stool studies (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG (he has weakness/palpitations and likely hypokalemia); CXR; BMP (potassium, bicarbonate, anion gap); Stool ova & parasites; Stool Giardia antigen; Stool culture & multiplex pathogen panel with fecal elastase; Fecal leukocytes/occult blood & C. difficile; CBC; a micronutrient panel (B12, vitamin D, magnesium, albumin); TSH/free T4; and celiac serology (tTG-IgA with total IgA).

criticalActions.item: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites

coreActions.item: Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel +/- fecal elastase
Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin

bonusActions.item: Repeat O&P x3 or repeat antigen given intermittent cyst shedding

penalties.item: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing
CT abdomen or colonoscopy first-line before basic stool studies

requiredTests: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites
optionalTests: Stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel +/- fecal elastase
Fecal leukocytes/occult blood and C. difficile toxin
Repeat O&P x3 or repeat antigen given intermittent cyst shedding

orderSensitiveActions: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics or steroids before stool testing

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own 12-lead EKG, obtained because of his weakness and palpitations. Describe your findings and tie them to his presentation.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus rhythm with flattened/inverted T waves, PROMINENT U waves (esp. V2–V3), ST depression, and a long apparent QU interval — the hallmark of HYPOKALEMIA; recognizes this is his K of 2.9 from GI losses and confers arrhythmia risk. CREDIT: identifies U waves / flat T waves as the key abnormality (7); attributes them to hypokalemia from the diarrhea and links to K 2.9 / palpitations (5); systematic read with rate/rhythm and no acute ischemia (3). PENALTY: missing the U waves or calling the ST/T changes acute ischemia (-3); ignoring the electrolyte cause (-2).
keyFindings: prominent U waves (especially V2–V3) with flattened T waves and ST depression
long apparent QU interval consistent with hypokalemia; no acute ischemia
criticalFindings: Hypokalemic EKG: prominent U waves with flat T waves and ST depression from a potassium of 2.9 due to GI losses.
commonMistakes: mistaking the U waves for part of the T wave and missing hypokalemia
over-calling the ST/T changes as acute ischemia
failing to connect the EKG to the diarrheal potassium losses
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm at ~88 bpm with flattened T waves, prominent U waves best seen in V2–V3, mild ST depression, and a long apparent QU interval — the classic EKG of hypokalemia, matching his measured potassium of 2.9 from GI (diarrheal) losses. This is not acute ischemia; the importance is that the hypokalemia (compounded by low-normal magnesium) raises his arrhythmia risk and must be repleted as part of management.

criticalActions: Hypokalemic EKG: prominent U waves with flat T waves and ST depression from a potassium of 2.9 due to GI losses.
coreActions: prominent U waves (especially V2–V3) with flattened T waves and ST depression
long apparent QU interval consistent with hypokalemia; no acute ischemia

penalties.label: misses U waves / over-calls ischemia / ignores the electrolyte cause

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes low yield in this presentation and absence of free air (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-3).
keyFindings: no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in this presentation
criticalFindings: No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion on a normal film
ordering it reflexively without a clear indication
steps.idealAnswer: Normal chest film: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, and no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in a non-toxic patient with chronic diarrhea but reassuring against a surgical abdomen.

criticalActions: No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
coreActions: no free air under the diaphragm; low yield in this presentation

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Stool shows a positive Giardia antigen with cysts/trophozoites on O&P, absent fecal leukocytes, no occult blood, negative bacterial culture, and negative C. difficile. His BMP shows K 2.9 with a normal-anion-gap acidosis, his EKG shows U waves, his B12 and vitamin D are low, his TSH is up at 9.8, and his tTG-IgA is weakly positive (24) with a normal total IgA. Commit to a diagnosis, state how you discriminate it from celiac disease, and account for the other abnormalities.
steps.rubric: Commits to GIARDIASIS as the unifying diagnosis (positive antigen + cysts/trophozoites, non-inflammatory pattern, clear water exposure) (4). Discriminates from celiac: the directly positive parasite testing makes giardiasis the answer, while the weakly positive tTG and malabsorption can be SECONDARY to Giardia mucosal injury — so the discriminating test is to TREAT, then RE-CHECK tTG-IgA (and consider duodenal biopsy) only if symptoms or serology persist (3). Accounts for the multi-system findings as ONE malabsorptive process: hypokalemic non-anion-gap acidosis and U waves from GI losses, low B12/vitamin D and macrocytic anemia from small-bowel malabsorption (PPI compounds B12), and the rising TSH from impaired levothyroxine absorption (worsened by taking it with coffee) (3). Names residual uncertainty — celiac not fully excluded until post-treatment re-check (bonus).
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: Giardiasis. The positive Giardia antigen plus cysts/trophozoites on O&P with a non-inflammatory pattern (absent fecal leukocytes, no occult blood, negative culture/C. difficile) and the untreated-stream-water exposure make this the unifying answer. Discriminating it from celiac: giardiasis itself causes small-bowel mucosal injury that produces malabsorption and can transiently raise tTG-IgA, so the weakly positive tTG (24, normal total IgA) is likely secondary — the discriminating step is to treat the Giardia and RE-CHECK tTG-IgA afterward, pursuing duodenal biopsy only if symptoms or serology persist. The other abnormalities are one malabsorptive story: the hypokalemic, normal-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis with U waves on EKG reflects GI potassium and bicarbonate losses; the low B12 and vitamin D with macrocytic anemia reflect small-bowel malabsorption (his long-term PPI compounds the low B12); and the rising TSH (9.8) despite a recent dose increase reflects impaired absorption of levothyroxine, worsened by taking it with coffee/breakfast. Residual uncertainty: celiac disease is not fully excluded until the post-treatment re-check.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including treatment, the electrolyte/EKG issue, his other medications, and safe disposition with follow-up?
steps.rubric: Per MGH diarrhea/parasitic infection: first-line anti-Giardia therapy — tinidazole single dose or metronidazole 5–7 days (nitazoxanide alternative) (CANT-MISS,6); correct the hypokalemia and address the U waves — oral/IV potassium repletion AND replace magnesium (K will not correct with low Mg), with oral rehydration and bicarbonate/volume restoration (CANT-MISS,5); counsel strict alcohol avoidance with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction) (3); medication reconciliation — separate levothyroxine from coffee/food and re-check TSH after recovery, and recognize the long-term PPI's contribution to low B12 (3); repletion of B12 and vitamin D and temporary dairy avoidance for transient lactose intolerance (2); exposure/public-health counseling — water treatment (boil/filter/treat) and hand hygiene around the toddler (2); arrange SAFE DISCHARGE/disposition — outpatient management with close follow-up, return precautions for worsening weakness/palpitations, and a planned tTG-IgA re-check (and GI referral) if symptoms persist (CANT-MISS,4). PENALTY: empiric fluoroquinolone/antibacterial as if bacterial (-2); discharging without repleting potassium/magnesium in a symptomatic hypokalemic patient with U waves (-3); prescribing metronidazole/tinidazole without alcohol-avoidance counseling (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Treat the Giardia: tinidazole 2 g single dose (or metronidazole 250 mg TID for 5–7 days; nitazoxanide is an alternative). Correct the electrolyte problem driving the U waves: replete potassium (oral, or IV if symptomatic) AND replace magnesium first/concurrently because hypokalemia will not correct while magnesium is low; give oral rehydration and restore volume/bicarbonate. Counsel strict alcohol avoidance during and for 3 days after metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). Reconcile his medications: have him take levothyroxine on an empty stomach separated from coffee/food and re-check TSH once he has recovered, and recognize that the long-term PPI plus malabsorption explains the low B12. Replete B12 and vitamin D, and advise temporary dairy avoidance for transient post-Giardia lactose intolerance. Give exposure/public-health counseling: treat or boil backcountry water, hand hygiene, and care around the toddler granddaughter to prevent fecal-oral spread. Disposition: safe for outpatient management with close follow-up; give clear return precautions for worsening weakness, palpitations, or syncope; recheck the potassium and arrange a post-treatment tTG-IgA re-check with GI referral (and duodenal biopsy) if diarrhea or serology persist.

criticalActions.item: Correct hypokalemia (potassium AND magnesium repletion) plus rehydration, addressing the U waves
Arrange safe discharge/disposition: outpatient with close follow-up, return precautions, and post-treatment tTG-IgA re-check / GI referral if persistent

coreActions.item: Counsel alcohol avoidance with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction)
Medication reconciliation — separate levothyroxine from coffee/food, re-check TSH; recognize PPI's role in low B12
Replete B12/vitamin D and advise temporary dairy avoidance
Exposure/public-health counseling — water treatment and hand hygiene

penalties.item: Empiric antibacterial therapy as if bacterial gastroenteritis
Discharging without repleting potassium/magnesium in a symptomatic hypokalemic patient with U waves

requiredActions: Correct hypokalemia (potassium AND magnesium repletion) plus rehydration, addressing the U waves
Arrange safe discharge/disposition: outpatient with close follow-up, return precautions, and post-treatment tTG-IgA re-check / GI referral if persistent

Exposure/public-health counseling — water treatment and hand hygiene

Discharging without repleting potassium/magnesium in a symptomatic hypokalemic patient with U waves
consults: Gastroenterology (if symptoms or celiac serology persist after treatment)
steps.disposition: Outpatient with close follow-up and return precautions

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: fatigued, slightly thin, mildly pale; afebrile, no acute distress.
Mucous membranes slightly dry; skin turgor mildly reduced; no severe dehydration.
Abdomen: hyperactive bowel sounds with mild distension/bloating.
Abdomen: soft with mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.
Neuro: mildly diminished vibration sense at the great toes (consistent with B12 deficiency); no focal deficit.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 44M with type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, and chronic PPI use presents with about three weeks of greasy, foul-smelling, floating diarrhea, bloating, sulfur burps, cramps, and ~10 lb weight loss after a backcountry camping trip with untreated stream water. The diagnosis is Giardiasis, but it presents as a layered malabsorption syndrome: a competing celiac picture (steatorrhea, macrocytosis, low B12/vitamin D, weakly positive tTG, family history) that resolves toward giardiasis on direct stool testing, plus multi-system abnormalities — a hypokalemic non-anion-gap acidosis with U waves on his own EKG, micronutrient malabsorption, and a rising TSH from impaired levothyroxine absorption. It is reached by eliciting the focused exposure/medication history, the targeted exam, an integrated EKG read, and a directed stool/malabsorption workup while keeping celiac as a post-treatment re-check.
keyHistoryClues: Subacute (>1 week) greasy, foul-smelling, floating stools without blood or mucus
Marked bloating, flatulence, and sulfur/rotten-egg belching
Untreated mountain stream water on a backcountry trip ~4 weeks ago
~10 lb weight loss with new/worsened dairy intolerance suggesting malabsorption
Weakness, muscle cramps, and palpitations (hypokalemia) and a rising TSH despite a recent levothyroxine increase
Family history of celiac and chronic PPI use as confounders
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: fatigued, slightly thin, mildly pale; afebrile.
Mucous membranes slightly dry; skin turgor mildly reduced.
Abdomen: hyperactive bowel sounds with mild distension/bloating.
Abdomen: soft with mild diffuse tenderness; NO guarding, NO rebound, NO peritonism, NO organomegaly.
Neuro: mildly diminished vibration sense at the great toes.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the diabetes/hypothyroid/PPI comorbidities.
Elicit the focused history, including the water/outdoor exposure, dietary triggers, weakness/palpitations, family celiac history, and levothyroxine timing only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam to reveal a soft, non-peritoneal, bloated abdomen, mild dehydration, pallor, and a B12-type sensory finding.
Build a differential led by Giardiasis with celiac disease as a genuine competing malabsorptive diagnosis, plus IBD, and name three mechanisms of malabsorptive diarrhea.
Order a focused workup (stool Giardia antigen/O&P, BMP, fecal markers, micronutrients, TSH, celiac serology) and read the patient's own EKG as integrated (hypokalemia with U waves).

Commit to Giardiasis, discriminate it from celiac (treat then re-check tTG), unify the multi-system labs, and manage with anti-Giardia therapy, potassium/magnesium repletion, medication reconciliation, exposure counseling, and safe outpatient disposition.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on giardiasis and ignoring the competing celiac/malabsorption picture
Empiric antibacterial therapy as if bacterial gastroenteritis
Missing the U waves on the EKG or failing to replete potassium AND magnesium
Prescribing metronidazole/tinidazole without counseling alcohol avoidance
Sending a single O&P and stopping when negative despite intermittent cyst shedding
Over-reading the weakly positive tTG as definite celiac rather than re-checking after treatment
managementPearls: Replete potassium AND magnesium — hypokalemia will not correct while magnesium is low
Separate levothyroxine from coffee/food and re-check TSH; recognize the PPI's role in low B12
Treat the Giardia, then re-check tTG-IgA before committing to celiac disease
criticalWorkupItems: Stool Giardia antigen (EIA) and/or stool ova & parasites
criticalInterpretationItems: Hypokalemic EKG with prominent U waves and ST depression from GI potassium losses.
No acute cardiopulmonary process on CXR; no free air (low yield).
criticalManagementItems: Arrange safe discharge/disposition with follow-up and post-treatment tTG-IgA re-check
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Hypokalemic EKG: prominent U waves with flat T waves and ST depression.
No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Hypokalemic EKG: prominent U waves with flat T waves and ST depression from a potassium of 2.9 due to GI losses.
No acute cardiopulmonary process; no free air.
imageInterpretationPearls: When a diarrheal patient is weak with palpitations, look for the hypokalemia signature — flat T waves and prominent U waves — and tie it to the measured potassium.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Normalizes embarrassing symptoms (gas, odor) to put the patient at ease
Asks permission before sensitive questions (alcohol, sexual history)
Explains the diagnosis and treatment in patient-friendly language
Checks understanding and counsels on exposure prevention and follow-up
